video_title: 140 Companies Just Declared War on Circle - The Chopping Block
gist: The Chopping Block panel (host, Tom, Tarun of Gauntlet, and guest Jordy of Selini) dissects MicroStrategy/Strategy's stressed Stretch preferred and Michael Sailor's new 'digital credit capital framework,' then debates OpenUSD (OSD) — a 140-partner stablecoin consortium led by ex-Bridge founder Zack Abrams — as a challenge to the Circle/Tether duopoly, before lighter takes on the Anom memecoin revival and the ENS DAO governance blow-up.

insights.claim: MicroStrategy's Stretch preferred, targeted at a $100 price, collapsed into the 80-cent territory and hit a low of $71 last week, signaling deep market doubt about the strength of Sailor's capital stack.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A preferred trading far below par flags rising perceived credit risk in the MSTR capital structure and is the tell to watch for further stress.
insights.quote: It hit a low of $71 last week relative to the $100 that it was historically trading at.

insights.claim: MSTR is down about 30% in five days, roughly 82% off its July 2025 peak, and its MNAV is now well below one, meaning the stock trades below the value of the Bitcoin it holds in reserve.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A sub-1 MNAV removes the premium that fuels the accretive-issuance flywheel, undercutting the whole 'Bitcoin per share' thesis.
insights.quote: The MNAV is now well below one, meaning that it's now trading below the value of all the Bitcoin held in reserve.

insights.claim: Michael Sailor is repositioning MicroStrategy as a 'digital credit company' rather than a Bitcoin treasury vehicle (DAT), whose job is to issue attractive digital credit backed by transforming risky Bitcoin into financial assets.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: The rebrand shifts the bull case from NAV premium to credit issuance, changing which metrics (dividend coverage, buybacks) matter for valuing MSTR.
insights.quote: He is a digital credit company. His job is to issue very attractive forms of digital credit and do this kind of capital transformation from risky bitcoin into this beautiful uh menagerie of financial assets that are backed by bitcoin.

insights.claim: Sailor is building a larger $2.5 billion cash cushion for dividends and interest, extending dividend coverage to roughly 18 months from about 12, and hiking the Stretch dividend from 11.5% to 12% in an unusually large single move.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Bigger coverage and a jumbo rate hike are defensive moves that buy time; investors should watch whether the cash cushion depletes on buybacks.
insights.quote: 2.5 billion that he's going to be setting aside for just dividends and interest which would give them roughly 18 months of dividend coverage.

insights.claim: Sailor announced buybacks of up to $1 billion of other preferreds (Stretch prioritized) plus up to another $1 billion of MSTR below par, and may sell up to about 2.5% of Bitcoin holdings (~$1.25 billion) to fund the defense.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Willingness to sell Bitcoin beyond dividend coverage is the key new risk vector for both MSTR holders and the broader BTC bid.
insights.quote: He announced that he may sell Bitcoin in order to fund this if necessary and something on the order of about 2.5% of holdings are up for sale potentially if he needs to monetize some of the Bitcoin.

insights.claim: On the framework announcement Sailor sold about $1.15 billion via MSTR ATM sales and Stretch rallied roughly 12% from $75 to $84 (yield ~14%) while MSTR rose 12%, showing renewed confidence in the recapitalized structure.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: The Stretch price recovery is the market's real-time verdict on the framework and a cleaner confidence gauge than MSTR itself.
insights.quote: Stretch went from $75 up to $84 yesterday, an increase of about 12%.

insights.claim: Jordy calls the digital credit framework a masterful stroke and frames Stretch as fundamentally a long-term bet that Bitcoin compounds faster than the ~11-12% dividend, viable as long as Sailor avoids liquidation before that plays out.
insights.speaker: Jordy (guest, Selini)
insights.so_what: Reframes Stretch as a solvency-plus-time trade: it works if BTC's long-run growth beats the coupon and Sailor stays liquid for a few years.
insights.quote: It's even in essence, it's a long-term bet that Bitcoin will grow at more than 11 12%. Which I don't think is a bad bet.

insights.claim: Jordy argues the Luna-style death-spiral comparison for Stretch is overblown because Luna was worthless and infinitely printable while Bitcoin cannot be printed and always has fiat buyers at some price, so he expects the panic to fade within six months.
insights.speaker: Jordy (guest, Selini)
insights.so_what: A credible counter to the bear thesis; if correct, current MSTR/Stretch weakness is a buying opportunity rather than the start of collapse.
insights.quote: Luna was worthless and you could print infinite Luna while Bitcoin you cannot print any Bitcoin

insights.claim: Tarun maintains his 'Luna for Suits' skepticism, warning that Sailor must constantly sell at-the-money MSTR and that opening Bitcoin sales beyond dividend coverage risks a 'turtles all the way down' problem of finding the marginal MSTR buyer.
insights.speaker: Tarun (Gauntlet)
insights.so_what: Flags the structural fragility: the recap depends on perpetual dilution and index inclusion, both of which weaken as market cap falls.
insights.quote: if they start actually selling, it does feel like it's turtles all the way down cuz like how who's the who's the marginal MSTR buyer at that point?

insights.claim: The panel notes MSTR's ability to keep selling at-the-money shares depends on index membership, since index rebalance flows provide daily ADV support that disappears if its market cap drops out of the indices.
insights.speaker: Tarun (Gauntlet)
insights.so_what: Index removal would be a step-change negative, cutting off a captive buyer for the ATM issuance that funds the whole structure.
insights.quote: if they lose their place in an index in terms of like their market cap goes down too much, they're going to have a lot of trouble selling the at the monies as well.

insights.claim: The panel identifies a reflexive doom loop: if Stretch depegs Sailor sells more Bitcoin, which weakens BTC and MSTR, forcing MSTR buybacks, so the liabilities can compound and get away from him despite his stated intent to defend Stretch.
insights.speaker: Tarun (Gauntlet)
insights.so_what: The reflexivity, not the raw size of Bitcoin sales, is the real tail risk to monitor if Stretch slips back toward 60.
insights.quote: It's not a lot, but it is reflexive. That's the problem.

insights.claim: The panel likens Stretch's marketing to Terra's Anchor — citing the AI-generated woman promising she'll 'never be stressed again' and an image of Sailor escaping a sinking Titanic — as 'anti-cult Bitcoin' iconography that undercuts confidence.
insights.speaker: Tarun (Gauntlet)
insights.so_what: Marketing that echoes a failed yield product is a soft sentiment signal that colors institutional perception of Stretch.
insights.quote: there's something about the way Stretch is marketed that feels a lot like Anchor

insights.claim: Sailor is currently the marginal flow in the Bitcoin market, with Goldman's desk reporting that much of what they see is clients positioning around when MicroStrategy will buy or sell.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: When one actor is the marginal buyer/seller, Bitcoin's near-term price is a function of MSTR mechanics, not organic demand.
insights.quote: today at the uh this this Goldman Um, if Gam was talking about how so much of what he sees on on his desk is people positioning around around micro strategy

insights.claim: Bitcoin fell from ~80K back to ~60K largely because front-runners of Sailor's predictable monthly ~$2 billion buys on the 14th piled in and then dumped once it became clear the buy would not repeat.
insights.speaker: Jordy (guest, Selini)
insights.so_what: Explains the drawdown as a positioning unwind and implies the front-running bid is now gone, removing a prior source of support.
insights.quote: every month he was buying two billion on the 14th and you just kind of knew it was going to happen.

insights.claim: The spot Bitcoin ETF complex just saw roughly $5 billion of outflows — the single largest run of outflows so far — leaving no obvious marginal buyer with retail, ETFs, and Sailor all absent.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A demand vacuum across retail, ETFs, and MSTR means the market needs a regime change to find fresh firepower.
insights.quote: outside of strategy, the ETF complex has seen like 5 billion of outflows, like the the single largest run of outflows that we've seen so far in the ETF complex

insights.claim: The bear case flagged is that equities do a double top and pull back into the midterms, with the possibility of a rate hike this year worsening the cheap-leverage trade that MicroStrategy relies on.
insights.speaker: Jordy (guest, Selini)
insights.so_what: A rate hike inverts Sailor's borrow-cheap-buy-Bitcoin trade and pressures the risk assets Strategy taps for capital.
insights.quote: now it looks like okay maybe there's actually going to be like a rate hike this year

insights.claim: The panel argues the destruction of private credit weakens demand for Stretch-like instruments, and notes Sailor markets himself as distinct from private credit yet is ultimately courting the same buyers.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Contagion from private-credit stress could bleed into Stretch demand since the end buyers overlap.
insights.quote: the destruction of private credit I think also just kind of weakens the demand for this kind of stuff

insights.claim: Jordy says the slam-dunk Bitcoin buy is not a price level but a moment — after equities have puked and a deleveraging has cleared the macro headwind — and disclosed buying Stretch under $80 to hold toward the high 80s.
insights.speaker: Jordy (guest, Selini)
insights.so_what: A concrete playbook: wait for a deleveraging washout rather than chasing a price, with Stretch under 80 as an actionable entry.
insights.quote: I don't think it's about price. I think it's either when like stocks have kind of puked and you're like, okay, there's no macro headwind here.

insights.claim: Jordy says the ideal time to buy Bitcoin is when the market again starts worrying about the dollar's value, but expects that catalyst is at least months away.
insights.speaker: Jordy (guest, Selini)
insights.so_what: Ties the next durable Bitcoin bid to a dollar-debasement narrative, giving a macro trigger to watch rather than a price.
insights.quote: the really really good time to buy is when people start worrying about the the dollar's value again, but that's not going to be probably for at least the months.

themes.theme: OpenUSD consortium vs the stablecoin duopoly

insights.claim: OpenUSD (OSD) is a new no-fee stablecoin governed by a ~140-partner consortium with no single company at the center, led by CEO Zack Abrams, co-founder of Bridge (acquired by Stripe), with free mint/redeem, no volume caps, and yield accruing to whoever mints.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A credible, zero-fee, broadly-owned stablecoin is the first structurally serious challenge to the Circle/Tether duopoly.
insights.quote: There doesn't seem to be a single company behind it, but rather a true consortium of something like 140 partners.

insights.claim: OpenUSD's partner list spans Visa, Mastercard, Stripe, Adyen, Fiserv, BlackRock, BNY Mellon, ICE, Google, Samsung, IBM, Shopify, Coinbase, Solana, Ripple, Polygon, Aptos, Plasma and Stellar, while Circle, Tether/USDT, PayPal and Binance are notably absent.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: The roster and the incumbents' absence define the battle lines; who is missing is as informative as who is in.
insights.quote: notably absent from this list were Circle, USDT, PayPal, each of which have their own stable coins.

insights.claim: Circle fell roughly 7-8% and Coinbase 5-6% on the OpenUSD announcement, reflecting that Coinbase's stock is heavily tied to the interest payments it receives from Circle for USDC held on its platform.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: The paired sell-off prices OSD as a direct threat to the Circle/Coinbase revenue-share economics, not just to Circle alone.
insights.quote: Coinbase was also down five six% today and obviously their stock price right now is heavily tied to Circle because they're getting these um interest payments

insights.claim: The panel argues consortium stablecoins historically fail without a muscular actor at the center — Center became just Circle/Coinbase and Libra only functioned because Facebook did the work — and with 140 vague partners it is unclear who does the heavy lifting for OSD.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: The central skeptic case: without an owner-operator driving integrations, OSD risks the diffuse-ownership failure mode of past consortia.
insights.quote: It does strike me that like it's really hard for consorcia to succeed uh without there being a very muscular presence at the center.

insights.claim: The panel warns that OSD's mint-based yield model invites farming: because a fully fungible token has no UTXO-style provenance, participants can circulate balances to game whatever revenue-share metric (AUM or KYC volume) the consortium picks.
insights.speaker: Tarun (Gauntlet)
insights.so_what: The chosen revenue-sharing rule is the make-or-break design variable; a poorly designed one gets arbitraged by the savviest member.
insights.quote: these revenue sharing agreements are actually the key thing of like whether this is real or not

insights.claim: Haseeb argues stablecoins are structurally hard and sticky with strong network effects, so zero mint/redeem fees is only a marginal edge versus incumbents' liquidity, acceptance and ubiquity — meaning Tether keeps exchanges and emerging markets while Circle keeps DeFi and its BD-won integrations.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Predicts incumbents defend their core turf and OSD's fee advantage is not enough to dislodge entrenched liquidity moats.
insights.quote: going to okay, zero mint, zero redeem fees is an advantage but not that much of an advantage relative to just the liquidity to the acceptance to the ubiquity

insights.claim: Jordy counters that the real stablecoin rake is the interest earned on holdings, not mint/redeem fees, and that firms are increasingly tired of holding stables and earning nothing while Tether keeps the lion's share, leaving room for a lower-rake entrant.
insights.speaker: Jordy (guest, Selini)
insights.so_what: If holders can be paid more of the float yield, a lower-rake consortium coin could win balances even without dislodging trading liquidity.
insights.quote: the rake is not really the mint redeem the m the rake is the is the interest on holding

insights.claim: The panel frames OpenUSD as best suited to expanding the stablecoin market into new B2B, interbank-settlement and 'stablecoin sandwich' rails (like Tempo's pitch) rather than winning existing turf such as Binance perp pairs or DeFi, because consortia free-ride and won't fund risky market development.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Suggests OSD's realistic TAM is greenfield B2B payments, so watch adoption among non-crypto partners, not exchange share.
insights.quote: you don't use a consortia to go open up a new continent. You use an explorer on a ship that's, you know, venture financed

insights.claim: Haseeb argues the stablecoin market is tiny relative to members like BlackRock or BNY Mellon (Circle is only a ~$20 billion company), so the market must be expanded — precisely the market-development work at which free-riding consortia underperform a single owner who internalizes the gains.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Explains why a huge-partner consortium may still stall: scale mismatch means members won't shoulder uncompensated expansion risk.
insights.quote: the stable coin market is tiny maybe to you know to circle it's big but circle is a $20 billion company

insights.claim: The panel notes the single unifying incentive across OSD's 140 members may simply be a shared desire to prevent Tether or Circle from owning the stablecoin layer, making it a 'commoditize your complement' play rather than a bid to own the asset.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Reframes member motivation as defensive: they may support OSD enough to deny incumbents a monopoly but not enough to drive adoption.
insights.quote: maybe I'm not going to own the stable coin but I just don't want those guys to own the stable coin

insights.claim: The panel warns OSD's headline partner count may be as hollow as Libra's, recalling that many Libra 'partners' had signed only watery best-effort LOIs without board sign-off, and that every big firm's 'crypto guy' needs an annual win so signing up is a default, not a signal.
insights.speaker: Tarun (Gauntlet)
insights.so_what: Discount the 140-name roster; real commitment, not press-release partnerships, is what to verify before treating OSD as inevitable.
insights.quote: each of these firms needs to have a crypto guy and that crypto guy needs a win every year

insights.claim: Coinbase is characterized as having two masters — it joined OSD but remains heavily incentivized to keep pushing USDC given its equity stake, so it likely won't promote OSD at the margin, and this fragmentation actually favors the incumbents.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A half-committed Coinbase blunts OSD's crypto-distribution edge and helps Circle retain USDC dominance.
insights.quote: Coinbase obviously has two masters here. They're very incentivized to continue pushing USDC

insights.claim: Tether CEO Paolo publicly praised OpenUSD as a real 'player two' — the same welcome he gave Libra — which the panel reads as his recurring bit for every new competitor, though this is the first one others also call a genuine rival.
insights.speaker: Tarun (Gauntlet)
insights.so_what: Even the dominant incumbent publicly acknowledging OSD as a real competitor is a sentiment marker that this launch is taken seriously.
insights.quote: he's also praising at the same time he's praising this new competitor. He's like, "Oh, this is going to be like a player two."

insights.claim: The panel notes PayPal's PYUSD has struggled for years and never surpassed its peak despite heavy incentives and PayPal's own distribution, implying weaker corporate consortium coins face even longer odds.
insights.speaker: Jordy (guest, Selini)
insights.so_what: PYUSD is the cautionary base rate: a well-distributed corporate stablecoin still failed to gain traction, tempering OSD optimism.
insights.quote: PYUSD. They've been trying to get that thing off the ground for years.

insights.claim: The White House signaled approval of OpenUSD, with crypto official Patrick Witt (under David Sacks) tweeting support, and the panel muses that Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent's interest in stablecoins for Treasury demand could make government backing a bullish catalyst.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Explicit US-government endorsement lowers regulatory risk for OSD and hints stablecoins are viewed as a Treasury-demand tool.
insights.quote: Patrick Dwit who's uh part of the White House uh crypto something... He tweeted like this is great.

insights.claim: The panel highlights a proliferation of competing stablecoin consortia — Robinhood is aligned with a separate consortium, USDG, rather than OpenUSD — raising the question of how many such coalitions the market can sustain.
insights.speaker: Tom
insights.so_what: Fragmentation across rival consortia dilutes each one's network effects and may hand the win back to focused incumbents.
insights.quote: there's like also this like competing consoria thing which is like also weird like coast theorem has to apply eventually.

insights.claim: Dan argues OpenUSD is easier to sell internally than Libra because stablecoins offer members straightforward, attributable Treasury-yield splits rather than nebulous token appreciation, and virtually every bank and financial institution now takes stablecoins seriously at the board or C-suite level.
insights.speaker: Dan
insights.so_what: If real Treasury-yield economics drive genuine board-level buy-in, OSD partner commitment could be more durable than Libra's was.
insights.quote: Stable coins are a much easier sell like crypto, right? Like Libra is a harder sell than this because Libra promised you some stake in the fees that was nebulous whereas this promises you like straight up treasury yield split attributably

insights.claim: A memecoin named for influencer Anom — which he did not launch — has rallied to roughly a $100 million market cap after he took 65% of the supply (from pump.fun creator fees) and began redistributing fees as airdrops to creators and influencers.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A $100M memecoin is the first sizable one in a long while, a tentative sign of returning speculative appetite in the trenches.
insights.quote: it's now got roughly a hundred million market cap on this memecoin which we have not seen in a long long time.

insights.claim: Jordy dismisses the memecoin narrative as WWE-style entertainment theater where 'organic' storylines are typically constructed, and suspects platform incentives (pump.fun promoting its new creator-fee feature) may be driving the excitement.
insights.speaker: Jordy (guest, Selini)
insights.so_what: Treat memecoin rallies as staged narrative events, not organic demand; the 'story' is a product feature, not a fundamental.
insights.quote: this is like the WWF I used to watch as a kid. You know, you got Hulk Hogan and this guy. This is like entertainment theater

insights.claim: The panel frames speculative fervor as inherently cyclical — pent-up demand periodically forces a stupid coin to speculate on, waves up, then dies — and observes that surviving memecoins now metastasize around a single main character (Anom, the White Whale coin, Binance Life).
insights.speaker: Tarun (Gauntlet)
insights.so_what: The durable memecoins are personality-anchored; watch the main character, not the token, to gauge staying power.
insights.quote: whenever a certain amount of time goes by, people do kind of like the the demand gets pent up for some stupid coin to speculate on

insights.claim: In the ENS DAO's vote to delegate treasury control to a foundation, Nick Johnson single-handedly blocked the proposal using roughly 50% of the voting supply without prior forum signaling, arguing a security council must remain as a backstop against compromise and constitutional violations.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A concrete case of one holder vetoing a DAO, fueling the narrative that token governance concentrates control in a few large holders.
insights.quote: He took all of his tokens, which is roughly 50% of the supply... and blocked the vote uh with just his single vote

insights.claim: The panel identifies a broader vibe shift against DAOs — citing MakerDAO's Rune and Aave's Stani recentralizing power — reframing DAOs as overly bureaucratic decentralization theater in favor of efficiency.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: A governance regime change toward founder-led efficiency could reprice DAO tokens whose value rested on a decentralization premium.
insights.quote: there's a vibe shift in hey Dow are overly complicated, overly bureaucratic. This was a little bit decentralization theater.

insights.claim: Tom argues the ENS outcome is token governance working exactly as advertised — concentrated in large holders like a libertarian Articles-of-Confederation federation — and that OpenUSD similarly resembles a thin federation rather than a democracy.
insights.speaker: Tom
insights.so_what: Reframes the ENS 'failure' as users misreading token voting as democratic; the mechanism did what it says on the tin.
insights.quote: we explicitly said we're doing token governance and that's what this looks like

insights.claim: The panel's optimistic take is that AI agents voting on holders' behalf could finally make representative-democracy and DAO governance viable by removing the time cost of participation — a revival of the original DFINITY pitch.
insights.speaker: Dan
insights.so_what: AI-proxied voting is a speculative catalyst that could revive DAO governance models and the tokens tied to them.
insights.quote: if I have an AI doing kind of representing me in Dows or in structures that... just tell the AI to take care of voting for me... That might make these things more viable.
